points: New York is the epicenter of BESS restrictions.

As of the June 2026 tracker refresh, New York had 86 active local BESS moratoriums (124 tracked; Carina's June refresh reclassified 20 records after verifying expiration dates), still more than 80% of every active BESS moratorium in the U.S. Westchester remains among the most-restricted counties. The coalition tracks 5 towns with an active moratorium (Bedford, Cortlandt, New Castle, the Village of Ossining, and Pound Ridge); Cortlandt's moratorium runs through July 31, 2026 per LL 7-2025 (certified law text), and Pound Ridge's through Sept 24, 2026. Mamaroneck Village concluded its moratorium drafting period and adopted a permanent restrictive code (LL 7-2026: Tier 1 ≤40/80 kWh; >80 kWh prohibited). Several towns have outright bans (Mount Pleasant, Mount Kisco, Somers).
Local control is decisive for standalone projects.

The State Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES) has no jurisdiction over standalone battery storage. A standalone BESS of any size must be permitted locally, so an active town moratorium or ban is binding with no state workaround. Only storage co-located with a solar/wind project ≥25 MW can use ORES to override local zoning.

A behind-the-meter (BTM) battery serves the property it sits on, a home or business system behind the customer's utility meter, typically Tier 1 (≤80 kWh). A front-of-the-meter (FTM) system is a standalone, grid-serving project that sells stored power to the grid, the storage that actually delivers county- and transmission-level value. The distinction matters because many Westchester codes that nominally "allow" BESS permit only BTM systems (Tier-1-only or "on-site use only" rules), which provides no pathway for grid-scale storage. Each town card now shows its storage pathway: FTM pathway, BTM only, no pathway, or undefined.

Senate Bill S5506 (Kavanagh) and Assembly Bill A8378 (Levenberg) would expand ORES to standalone storage. Introduced Feb 2025, still in the Energy & Telecommunications Committee when the Legislature adjourned its 2026 session in June, not passed either chamber, so it cannot advance before the Legislature reconvenes in January 2027. Several Westchester municipalities (e.g., Yorktown) passed resolutions opposing it.
Most moratoriums are temporary, but some harden into bans.

Moratoriums are usually a pause while a town writes zoning; a large NY cluster expires mid-to-late 2026. In Westchester, Mount Pleasant and Mount Kisco have moved from moratorium to effective bans, while Port Chester adopted permanent regulations instead. The next several months are the highest-leverage window to shape the permanent rules.

snapshot: 75% Red, 25% Yellow, Low capacity (ConEd/NYSEG hosting-capacity map)
§178-9, Tier 1 (≤600 kWh) by-right; Tier 2 only by special-use permit.
summary: Briarcliff Manor regulates battery storage under Village Code Chapter 178: small Tier 1 systems (≤600 kWh) are permitted by-right in every district with a building permit, while large Tier 2 systems (>600 kWh) are allowed only through a Board-of-Trustees special-use permit plus Planning Board site-plan review. So the Village has a full tiered framework rather than an outright Tier 2 ban.

In practice the Tier 2 pathway is heavily constrained, a 5-acre minimum site in residential zones, large setbacks, and 0.1-mile avoidance buffers around schools, healthcare, senior housing, and municipal buildings, which, combined with low grid hosting capacity, makes utility-scale storage difficult to site. A standing Village BESS Committee has issued initial recommendations and continues to refine the rules, so there is an open, substantive process to engage.

How restrictive: Tier 2 is not banned, but siting rules make it very hard: 5-acre minimum lot in residential zones, 50 ft property-line / 100 ft building setbacks, and 0.1-mile "avoidance" zones around schools, healthcare, senior-living, and municipal facilities.

Active review: A Village BESS Committee (Mayor, Village Manager, Engineer, DPW, Counsel, Fire & Police chiefs, Sustainability Advisory Council) issued Initial Recommendations refining Tier 1 and Tier 2 standards, the code is being actively worked.
committees: Board of Trustees, issues Tier 2 special-use permits and adopts any code amendments.

towns.name: Town of North Castle
towns.scope: Restrictive, Type 1 (utility, >600 kWh) & Type 2 (commercial, 80–600 kWh) prohibited town-wide; only Type 3 (≤80 kWh residential) possibly allowed (LL 4-2024)
towns.subtitle: Northern Westchester, Armonk, North White Plains, Banksville
snapshot: 25% Red, 75% Yellow, Moderate capacity (ConEd/NYSEG hosting-capacity map)
LL 4 of 2024, commercial and utility-scale BESS prohibited town-wide.
summary: North Castle adopted Local Law 4 of 2024 on August 14, 2024, which redefined "public utility" to exclude battery energy storage and amended the Town Code’s permitted-use sections to prohibit Type 1 (utility, >600 kWh) and Type 2 (commercial, 80–600 kWh) BESS in the residential and IND-A industrial zones, and to prohibit all BESS in the CB-A and GB business districts. Only Type 3 residential systems (≤80 kWh) are left unaddressed, neither permitted nor prohibited.

The Westchester County Planning Board reviewed the measure (Referral NOC 24-008) and called it an effective ban on Types 1 and 2, formally disagreeing with a near-total ban and recommending the NYSERDA Model Law instead. This confirms North Castle is a restrictive, near-ban jurisdiction, an engagement target to replace the prohibition with a real, well-sited tiered pathway, not a model to emulate.

BESS types (North Castle numbering): Type 1 = utility (>600 kWh or grid-serving); Type 2 = commercial (80–600 kWh, on-site only, no sale of excess to the grid); Type 3 = residential (≤80 kWh). Type 1 & 2 are prohibited; Type 3 is neither permitted nor prohibited.

County pushback: The Westchester County Planning Board (Referral NOC 24-008, July 1, 2024) found the law would "effectively ban BESS systems of Types 1 and 2," disagreed that a near-total ban is appropriate, and urged the Town to adopt the NYSERDA Model Law and clarify Type 3 status.
flags: Primary sources confirmed (7/2/26): Town of North Castle Local Law 4 of 2024 (adopted 8/14/24) and Westchester County Planning Board Referral NOC 24-008 (7/1/24). The County itself characterized the law as an effective ban on Type 1 & Type 2 BESS. RESOLVED 7/3/26: the workgroup GIS sheet now lists North Castle as a BESS ban (row updated 7/2/26), matching this dashboard's reading of LL 4-2024, the earlier "BESS allowed (3 Tiers)" entry was corrected. Verified in the workgroup GIS sheet. Classification note: kept as "Restrictive" because Type 3 (≤80 kWh) residential systems are not expressly prohibited; the workgroup could reasonably treat this as a "Ban" given the County’s "effective ban" finding.

steps: Prioritize the towns mid-drafting, Cortlandt, New Castle, Pound Ridge, Bedford, Peekskill, Mamaroneck (Village), and Yonkers, where a permanent law is actively being written. Input now shapes the actual text.
Engage Greenburgh on its amendments. It already has a BESS law and is revising it via the Planning Board, an open, substantive process.
Get ahead in Rye Brook. With no moratorium yet, it is the best chance to help craft a balanced ordinance before a reactive ban.
Check Harrison and Yorktown's next moves. Both pauses lapsed; confirm whether permanent BESS codes are coming. Yorktown is also fighting state preemption (S5506/A8378).
Study Port Chester as a model. It chose permanent regulations over a ban, useful precedent and language for other towns.
Monitor the ban towns (Mount Pleasant, Mount Kisco). Bans can be revisited or challenged; watch for reconsideration or litigation.
Get on agendas in advance. Most boards require contacting the clerk/supervisor days ahead (Pound Ridge: 5 days, copy the Clerk). Public-comment periods are also available at each meeting.
Lead with the sustainability committees in each town for a “safe siting, not outright ban” message, and track the state bill S5506 / A8378.

items: Daily reconciliation: no new moratoriums, bans, extensions, adopted laws, or hearing notices. The workgroup GIS sheet is unchanged (latest rows dated 6/15–6/18/26); Carina remains on its June 2026 refresh (Westchester actives: Bedford, New Castle, Cortlandt, Yonkers, Peekskill). All 45 municipality statuses re-confirmed.

Cortlandt watch (moratorium expires 7/31/26 per certified LL 7-2025): the Town Board meets 7/21/26 — the key engagement date before expiration. No further extension or replacement code posted to the town Legal Notices page as of this run.
Peekskill: confirmed the >80 kWh (Tier 2) moratorium was extended into 2026 by unanimous Council vote; exact expiration still unposted — flag retained.
S5506/A8378 (ORES standalone-storage) still in committee, no movement.

Visual redesign: switched to the Inter typeface, refined the accent palette around the BCW navy, added a navy header band, restyled the section tabs, and gave cards and panels soft shadows and rounded corners.
Status boxes now use the full status color as their background with white text; removed all emojis sitewide.

Added a Successful Projects tab with photos and logos pulled from the CEAC deck, elaborating each Westchester solar-plus-storage project; moved the It's Already Working Here content out of the About tab.

Removed the Why Local Moratoriums Matter section and wove its key points into other tabs: local control / ORES and the temporary-moratorium window into Model Law, behind-the-meter vs. front-of-the-meter into About.
Renamed the status boxes to be BESS-specific (for example Towns With Active BESS Moratorium) and removed all em dashes sitewide.

Restructured the site into three selectable views below the status boxes, About Battery Storage (default), Model Law, and the Live Policy Tracker, to make the page generally informative, not just a tracker.

Added an About Battery Storage view (what BESS is, why storage matters for reliability/affordability/the clean-energy transition, local results, and safety) drawn from the CEAC Local Leaders briefing.

Replaced the Model Municipalities list with a Model Law view centered on the NYSERDA model law, with Croton-on-Hudson and Tarrytown as adopt-and-customize examples; removed the MODEL label from Greenburgh (its code is mid-amendment).

New weighted priority scoring system (time sensitivity 30, severity 25, grid capacity 20, population 15, receptivity 10), every town scored and ranked; top ten hold the ★ Priority label. Methodology documented in Verification & Confidence.
Added card sorting: Priority (default), A–Z, and earliest expiration.

Daily reconciliation vs the workgroup GIS sheet and town sources: no new moratoriums, bans, extensions, adopted laws, or hearing notices found. All 45 municipality statuses re-confirmed.

North Castle: resolved the standing sheet conflict, the workgroup GIS sheet (row updated 7/2/26) now records North Castle as a BESS ban, agreeing with the dashboard's LL 4-2024 analysis; classification stays RESTRICTIVE because Type 3 (≤80 kWh) residential systems remain unaddressed rather than prohibited.

Added a battery-shaped capacity spectrum to every card's At a Glance: a log-scale bar (1 kWh → 100 MWh) inside a battery outline showing what each capacity range gets, allowed by-right (green), special permit (blue), prohibited (red), paused (amber hatch), on-site/BTM-only (green hatch), or undefined (gray), with the town's actual kWh thresholds marked and Tier 1/2/3 tags on the segments. Tier thresholds vary by town, so segments encode real capacity ranges rather than tier numbers (North Castle's inverted Type numbering is annotated in its labels). Classifications derive from each entry's localLaw; solar-code and silent-code towns show as undefined.
Added a battery-gauge visual for grid hosting capacity on every card (fill level = capacity tier; colored segments = the ConEd/NYSEG map reading; hatched = NYSEG territory).

Added a behind-the-meter vs. front-of-the-meter classification (meterAccess) for all 45 municipalities, rendered as a 'Storage pathway' line in each card's At a Glance grid, with a BTM/FTM explainer added to 'Why Local Moratoriums Matter.' Classifications for solar-code and silent-code towns are provisional.

COMPREHENSIVE REVIEW PASS (full-site audit vs primary sources): Cortlandt CORRECTION (critical), the certified text of LL 7-2025 (filed with NYS, adopted 12/9/25) extends the moratorium through JULY 31, 2026, not Dec 31, 2026; the 6/30 change that trusted the hearing notice over the adopted law is reversed and the workgroup sheet's '07-2026' stands. Updated scope, snapshot, summary, flags, localLaw, state points, calendar deadline, and Priority #1.

Bedford: removed the internal contradiction between 'until 8/17/26' and 'extended through Jan 16, 2026', the entry now consistently reflects the LL 1-2026 chain (large-scale pause through 8/17/26, consistent with Carina's June listing). New Castle: scope/summary/flags rewritten to stop asserting both 'lapsed 5/31/26' and 'active through ~Aug 2026', status unresolved, flagged for Town Clerk verification. Somers: corrected misattribution, the ban that stopped East Point's 116 MW project was the Town of Carmel's (Putnam Co.), not a Somers code change; Somers' ban rests on §170-80.

Accuracy pass: completed the 'Silent Code' rename on card badges; restored the missing Yonkers ACTIVE-on-Carina flag and added the ≤600 kWh moratorium exemption; updated statewide figures to Carina's June refresh (86 active / 124 tracked); fixed Peekskill kW→kWh units and Harrison's Tier 1 scope typo (≤80 kWh); Mount Pleasant ban date corrected to fall 2025 (announced effective Oct 30, 2025) and its dead press-release link replaced; filled verified officials (Port Chester Mayor Luis Marino; Peekskill council names; Mount Kisco board) and flagged Larchmont Mayor Bauer's announced retirement (Nov 2026); filled the previously-empty Village of Ossining and Croton-on-Hudson cards; S5506/A8378 noted as unable to advance before Jan 2027 (session adjourned).

Dashboard upgrades: per-town At-a-Glance snapshot (grid hosting capacity, governing law, engagement window) now renders on every card; moratorium/deadline cards show an 'expires' countdown chip; footer tracker vintage is now data-driven; changelog dates render cleanly; calendar supports month-specific schedules (fixes phantom Harrison meetings); keyboard access for card toggles.

New Castle: verified via Westchester County Planning referral NWC-25-007 (10/28/25) that the proposed permanent law permits ONLY Tier 1 (≤80 kWh) and PROHIBITS Tier 2 & Tier 3 town-wide (the County objected to the ban). Corrected the scope line, which had described a permissive 3-tier framework; added the referral confirmation to snapshot/localLaw/flags. Status kept as Moratorium pending confirmation the Town Board has adopted the law, if adopted, reclassify to Restrictive/effective ban.

Scanned the County 239-M Referrals Hub for all BESS referrals and cross-checked: Mount Kisco (MTK-25-001) and Mount Pleasant (MTP-25-010) "BESS Prohibition" referrals corroborate their ban status; Cortlandt, Ossining Village, Mamaroneck Village, Larchmont, Yorktown, and Bedford referrals all consistent with current entries. Added confirming referral links to Mount Kisco, Mount Pleasant, New Castle, and Briarcliff (BMR-25-005).

North Castle: added primary-source detail from the adopted Local Law 4 of 2024 and Westchester County Planning Board Referral NOC 24-008 (both provided from email). LL 4-2024 (8/14/24) excludes BESS from "public utility" and prohibits Type 1 (>600 kWh) & Type 2 (80–600 kWh) in residential/IND-A zones and all BESS in the CB-A/GB business zones; the County called it an effective ban on Types 1 & 2 and recommended the NYSERDA Model Law. Corrected the Town Hall address (15 Bedford Road) and the earlier imprecise "systems >80 kW exempt" note.
Added the Westchester County Planning Department 239-M Referrals Hub (ArcGIS) as a project resource/source for tracking municipal BESS referrals.

North Castle RECLASSIFIED (major): moved from "BESS Allowed / model" to "Restrictive." A workgroup contact flagged that North Castle does not permit larger-scale BESS and received a 2024 ban referral; confirmed via the Examiner (8/27/24) that the Town adopted an 8/14/24 local law excluding BESS from the "public utility" definition, effectively barring utility-scale/standalone storage (household systems >80 kW exempt). The sheet’s "3 tiers allowed" came from §355-4 definitions, which only define, not permit, the tiers. Removed dr/model status. Conflict flagged for the workgroup to update the GIS sheet.

Deep-dived four restrictive codes and corrected contradictory entries: City of Rye (LL 2025-02 §98-124.1, Tier 1 <80 kWh only; new Mayor Josh Nathan since Jan 2026), Village of Larchmont (LL 2-2026, Tier 1 ≤600 kWh, Tier 2 prohibited), Town of North Salem (§250-13, on-site-only via public-utility exclusion), and Village of Rye Brook (LL 2-2025 §98-124.1, Tier 1 ≤80 kWh, corrected a prior "no ordinance on record" error). Added officials, meeting schedules, and primary-source links to each.

Mount Kisco: corrected the ban mechanism, it was adopted as filed Local Law 1-2025 (redefines "public utility" to exclude BESS and prohibits BESS in every district), not an informal zoning interpretation. Moratorium lapsed on adoption; existing systems grandfathered. Source: NYS filing receipt provided by the workgroup (Don Emerson, 7/2/26) and press coverage of the July 14, 2025 public hearing. Fixed a scope-line date typo (was "≈Aug 2026").
Category label "No BESS code" renamed to "Silent Code" per team request.
Village of Pelham: added a watch-flag noting a draft BESS local law referenced by the workgroup (6/29/26 meeting) but not yet publicly confirmed/adopted.
Confirmed Pound Ridge remains correctly excluded from the Priority list (behind-the-meter, limited transmission/grid-arbitrage value) per workgroup guidance and the 6/29/26 meeting.

Yorktown RECLASSIFIED (major): moved from “BESS Allowed” to “Restrictive BESS Code.” The workgroup GIS sheet (re-read 7/1/26) classifies Yorktown as “BESS limited to Tier 1 only,” and press coverage of the Town Board’s May 20 vote confirms LL 10-2025 (§300-81.5) prohibits new Tier 2 (>80 kWh) systems. Updated status, scope, snapshot, summary, flags, and localLaw.

Cortlandt deadline re-verified: LL 7-2025 extends the ALL-BESS moratorium through Dec 31, 2026 (confirmed via the Town’s legal notices / LL text). The workgroup sheet still notes “expires 07/31/26,” treated as a date-entry error; dashboard keeps 12/31/26.

Corrected a stale state.points claim that the coalition tracks '7 active moratorium towns', the actual current count is 4 (Bedford, Cortlandt, New Castle, Village of Ossining), with Mamaroneck Village and Pound Ridge now under permanent restrictive codes rather than active moratoriums.

hostingNote: Grid hosting capacity is a proxy read from the ConEd hosting-capacity map colors in the workgroup sheet: more green/yellow = more available capacity (better BESS siting value); orange = limited; red = constrained. NYSEG-territory towns aren't on that map. Confirm against the utility legend before relying on it.
priorityIntro: Ranked by the scored priority system (see How Priority Is Ranked in Verification & Confidence): urgency, restrictiveness, hosting capacity, population, and receptivity. NYSEG-territory municipalities are excluded (score 0). The top ten scores carry the ★ Priority label.
meterNote: Behind-the-meter (BTM) systems sit behind a customer's utility meter and serve the load on that property, home and business batteries, typically Tier 1. Front-of-the-meter (FTM) systems are standalone, grid-serving projects that store and sell power to the grid, the storage that delivers transmission-level value. Pathway classifications for solar-code and silent-code towns are provisional; confirm against the code text.
priorityMethod:
How Priority Is Ranked

Every municipality gets a 0–100 priority score; the top ten carry the ★ Priority label and a spot in the Priority Municipalities list. Weights reflect impact:

Urgency, 30 pts. Is there a live chance to affect the code? Active moratoriums score highest (base 24), with bonuses as expiration approaches (+6 within 60 days, +4 within 180, +2 within a year) and +6 where a moratorium just lapsed or a permanent law is in open drafting. Adopted-but-restrictive codes score 10, bans 8, silent codes 6, healthy codes 2.

Restrictiveness, 25 pts. How bad is the current rule? Outright ban 25; moratorium on all BESS 22; large-scale-only moratorium 18; adopted-but-effectively-blocking code 16; silent code 8; healthy code 0.

Hosting Capacity, 20 pts. From the ConEd hosting-capacity map: High 20, Moderate 13, Low 5. NYSEG territory is scored 0 overall, it isn't on the ConEd map and sits outside the coalition's grid focus, so those municipalities are excluded from the ranking.

Population, 15 pts. 2020 Census bands: ≥100k = 15, 50–100k = 13, 25–50k = 11, 10–25k = 8, 5–10k = 5, <5k = 3. Bigger municipalities mean more residents served and more precedent value.

Receptivity, 10 pts. Party registration data is uneven at the municipal level (and nearly all of Westchester leans Democratic), so this uses more direct signals: an active BESS study/draft process (+4), a sustainability, climate, or conservation body to ally with (+3), and rules not yet locked in, silent or moratorium status, since shaping a code in motion is easier than reopening an adopted one (+3).

Each town card lists its rank and score under At a Glance. Scores are recomputed whenever the underlying data changes.
